input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.content: I need to fix the benchmark config to properly target both runtimes. Let me update the program to use BenchmarkDotNet's built-in multi-runtime support.
data.reasoningText:
The restore succeeded. Now let me build and run the benchmarks. BenchmarkDotNet in Release mode will take a while, but I'll let it run. I need to make sure I'm using the right SDK. The machine has SDK 11.0 preview which should support both net9.0 and net10.0 targets.

Wait, I need to think about the BenchmarkDotNet config. When I use `AddJob(Job.Default)` twice, BenchmarkDotNet will run both jobs on the same runtime (whatever the project was built for). To actually compare net9.0 vs net10.0 runtimes, I need to specify the runtime for each job.


I should configure the jobs with specific runtime monikers or use the command-line approach with `--runtimes net9.0 net10.0` to properly benchmark across both frameworks. I'll reorganize the approach.

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet 0.14 doesn't recognize `net10.0` yet. I'll run benchmarks separately for each TFM using the host runtime.
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.x doesn't know about .NET 10 yet. I need a different approach. I'll run the benchmarks separately for each TFM and compare results manually. Or I can use a custom runtime configuration.

Actually, the simplest approach: I'll just build and run separate benchmark runs for each TFM, using `Job.Default` (which uses the host process runtime). I'll run once with `--framework net9.0` and once with `--framework net10.0`.

Let me update Program.cs back to simple usage and run twice.

data.content:
Here are the benchmark results comparing **.NET 9** vs **.NET 10**:

| Method | .NET 9 | .NET 10 | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AverageWordLength_Small** | 77.5 ns | 18.3 ns | **~4.2× faster** |
| **AverageWordLength_Medium** | 2,594 ns | 684.5 ns | **~3.8× faster** |
| **WordLengthHistogram_Small** | 117.7 ns | 45.4 ns | **~2.6× faster** |
| **WordLengthHistogram_Medium** | 4,739 ns | 1,689 ns | **~2.8× faster** |
| **UniqueWordCount_Small** | 290.5 ns | 258.4 ns | **~1.1× faster** |
| **UniqueWordCount_Medium** | 13,710 ns | 12,240 ns | **~1.1× faster** |

**Memory allocations** are identical across both runtimes.

**Summary:** .NET 10 delivers significant performance gains for `AverageWordLength` (~4×) and `WordLengthHistogram` (~2.7×), likely from improved `ReadOnlyCollection<T>` indexer devirtualization/inlining. `UniqueWordCount` sees a modest ~12% improvement. The upgrade looks beneficial across the board — no regressions detected.

> ⚠️ Note: The .NET 9 `AverageWordLength` and `AverageWordLength_Medium` had high error margins (possible contention on this shared machine), so I used the **median** for those comparisons. All .NET 10 numbers were very stable.
